questions.question: Explain the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm with an example. What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Requests are serviced in the order they arrive in the queue
Advantage: Simple to implement, fair (no starvation)
Disadvantage: Large total head movement, not optimized for seek time
Provide a worked example with a request queue showing total head movement calculation

questions.question: Compare SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms. Which provides more uniform wait time and why?
keyPoints: SCAN: Head moves in one direction servicing requests, then reverses direction
C-SCAN: Head services requests in one direction only, jumps back to start without servicing on return
C-SCAN provides more uniform wait time because it treats the disk as circular
In SCAN, requests near the middle get served more frequently than those at the edges

questions.question: Given a disk with 200 cylinders (0-199), request queue = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67}, and initial head position at 53, calculate total head movement for SSTF algorithm.
keyPoints: From 53: closest is 65 (dist=12)
From 65: closest is 67 (dist=2)
From 67: closest is 37 (dist=30)
From 37: closest is 14 (dist=23)
From 14: closest is 98 (dist=84)
From 98: closest is 122 (dist=24)
From 122: closest is 124 (dist=2)
From 124: closest is 183 (dist=59)
Total = 236 cylinders

questions.question: What is the difference between LOOK and SCAN algorithms?
keyPoints: SCAN travels to the physical end of the disk before reversing direction
LOOK only travels to the last pending request in each direction before reversing
LOOK saves unnecessary head movement by not going to disk ends
LOOK is a practical improvement over SCAN used in real operating systems

questions.question: List and briefly explain all six disk scheduling algorithms. Provide a comparison table.
keyPoints: FCFS: Service in arrival order; simple but inefficient
SSTF: Service closest request; efficient but may cause starvation
SCAN: Elevator algorithm; head moves end-to-end servicing requests
C-SCAN: One-direction only with jump back; uniform wait times
LOOK: Like SCAN but reverses at last request, not disk end
C-LOOK: Like C-SCAN but jumps between last and first requests
Comparison table covering starvation, performance, and uniformity
